que: In Recomposition, what is the primary goal?
options: replace testing entirely
re-run composables when observed state changes
skip state/data modeling
avoid understanding requirements

que: Which API/concept is most central to Recomposition?
options: recomposition
random utility class name
manual polling loop for all tasks
legacy XML-only patterns unrelated to this topic

que: What is a recommended best practice for Recomposition?
options: optimize before verifying correctness
ignore edge/error states
mix unrelated responsibilities in one function
read state as late as possible

que: Which is a common mistake when implementing Recomposition?
options: follow official docs for API usage
write focused tests for behavior
keep boundaries clear
mutating state repeatedly during composition

que: For production code, which approach fits Recomposition best?
options: Use Recomposition only as theory with no implementation
Use Recomposition while bypassing architecture boundaries
Use Recomposition with clear state flow and testable logic
Use Recomposition and suppress all warnings

que: A teammate asks where to start with Recomposition. What is best advice?
options: avoid feedback from tests/review
copy an unknown snippet without context
skip running the app after changes
Read official docs (https://developer.android.com/develop/ui/compose/mental-model) and implement a small sample

que: Which statement about Recomposition is most accurate?
options: Recomposition removes the need for any testing
Recomposition is most effective when paired with clear ownership of state/data
Recomposition is mainly about adding boilerplate
Recomposition should be applied identically in every layer

que: In a code review for Recomposition, what would you flag?
options: traceable data flow between layers
small pure functions with clear names
Code that hides failures instead of handling them explicitly
explicit modeling of UI/data states

que: Which scenario is the strongest example of using Recomposition correctly?
options: an implementation with no error states
a large refactor without tests
A small, working feature that demonstrates recomposition in context
a slide deck with no runnable code

que: To learn Recomposition deeply, what should you do next?
options: skip verification to save time
memorize definitions only
Build, test, profile (if needed), and iterate using Recomposition patterns
avoid official references
